title: "Violet Moon Problem v61.19"

  description: "At precisely 22:20, the Violet Moon artifact went missing from a locked display in the grand gallery. Four suspects (1, 2, 3, 4) had verified keycard entries near that time. Exactly one suspect lies in all their statements; the other three are fully truthful. Only one suspect is actually guilty. Each suspect gives four statements:

    Suspect 1:
      (1) 'If 2 is lying, then 3 must have stolen the Violet Moon.'
      (2) 'I left this gallery at 22:10, so I'm clearly not the thief.'
      (3) '4 never approached the display after 22:15.'
      (4) 'Either 2 stayed until 22:20, or 3 is lying—but not both.'

    Suspect 2:
      (1) '1 actually lingered until 22:25, contradicting that 22:10 story.'
      (2) 'I never touched the Violet Moon after 22:00, so I'm no suspect.'
      (3) 'If 3 is telling the truth, then 4 must be lying in every statement.'
      (4) 'Either 1’s timeline is false, or 3 is guilty—but never both.'

    Suspect 3:
      (1) 'I did not go near that display after 22:05.'
      (2) 'Either 2 is lying, or 1 left at 22:20— but not both.'
      (3) 'If 4 is telling the truth, then 2 cannot be the culprit.'
      (4) 'I am not guilty; logs show me in a different corridor at 22:15.'

    Suspect 4:
      (1) '3 was definitely at the display at 22:10, contradicting 3’s claim.'
      (2) 'If 1 is truthful, then 2 must be lying entirely.'
      (3) 'I am not guilty; logs place me in another wing at 22:15.'
      (4) 'Either 3 is lying or 2 was near the artifact after 22:00—but not both.'

  question: "Which suspect (1, 2, 3, or 4) stole the Violet Moon? Provide only a single digit."

answer: >
  explanation: "Predicate Logic:

  Let L(x) = 'x lies in all statements,' T(x) = 'x is fully truthful,' G(x) = 'x is guilty.'
  Exactly one L(x)=true, exactly one G(x)=true, and the other three T(x)=true.

  Case L(1):
    Then 1(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => if 2 is lying => 3 must be thief is not correct => no forced scenario
      (2) false => 1 did not leave 22:10 => might be present or guilty
      (3) false => 4 did approach after 22:15 => potential blame
      (4) false => 'Either 2 stayed 22:20 or 3 lying but not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 2,3,4 are truthful => times or blame lead to contradictions with 2(1) or 3(2) or 4(1). Typically fails.

  Case L(2):
    Then 2(1),(2),(3),(4) all false => 
      (1) false => 1 did not linger 22:25 => possibly 1’s 22:10 claim stands
      (2) false => 2 was near artifact after 22:00 => suspicious
      (3) false => if 3 truth => 4 must be lying in every statement is undone => no forced scenario
      (4) false => 'Either 1 timeline false or 3 is guilty not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,3,4 are truthful => 1(2) 'Left 22:10 => not culprit' => G(1)=false, 3(4) 'I am not guilty => logs corridor' => G(3)=false, 4(3) 'I am not guilty => G(4)=false => leaving G(2)=true. No contradiction.

  Case L(3):
    Then 3(1),(2),(3),(4) are false => 
      (1) false => 3 did approach after 22:05 => might be culprit
      (2) false => 'Either 2 lying or 1 left 22:20 not both' undone => confusion
      (3) false => if 4 is truth => 2 not culprit undone => 2 might be culprit
      (4) false => 3 might be culprit or logs are false. Meanwhile 1,2,4 truthful => times produce conflict with 2(2) if 2 is T. Contradiction.

  Case L(4):
    Then 4(1),(2),(3),(4) all false => 
      (1) false => 3 was not at display 22:10 => 3’s claim stands
      (2) false => if 1 is truth => 2 is lying undone => no forced scenario
      (3) false => 4 might be guilty
      (4) false => 'Either 3 is lying or 2 near artifact after 22:00 not both' undone => might be both or neither
    Meanwhile 1,2,3 are truthful => 1(2) stands => G(1)=false, 2(2) 'never after 22:00 => G(2)=false, 3(4) 'I not guilty => G(3)=false => that leaves G(4)=true. No contradictions.

  The consistent scenario is L(2)=> G(2) or L(4)=> G(4). Checking 3(3) 'If 4 is truth => 2 not culprit' means if 4 is T => 2= not culprit => contradicts G(2). So if L(2), 4 is T => 2 not culprit is contradiction => fails. Then L(4) => G(4) stands. The culprit is suspect 4.

  final: '4'"
